monogr.title: The Conscious Lovers;

A Comedy, in Five Acts; By Sir Richard Steele. As Performed at the Theatres Royal, Covent Garden and Drury Lane. Printed under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald.

quotation.p: All quotation marks and apostrophes have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Quotation marks that indicate block quotes have been silently eliminated.

hyphenation.p: Any dashes occurring in line breaks have been removed.
All hyphens have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
em-dashes have been recorded originally using the —

normalization.p: Page numbers appear at the beginning of each page, no matter where originally placed.
Compositors' catchwords and running titles have been silently eliminated.
& has been used for the ampersand sign.
All other characters, those with accents, non-breaking spaces, etc., have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Writing in other hands appearing on these manuscripts has been indicated as such, the content recorded in brackets.

category.catDesc: author of main text and of works reviewed, if relevant, using LOC authority name headings.

list.item: Corrected titles in teiHeader to remove all items listed as NOT to be used in the Encoding Decisions, Header section, which I also updated to make more clear.
Added myself (with xml:id) as technical editor to the titleStmt

head.bibl:
Elizabeth Inchbald
REMARKS [on The Conscious Lovers]."Remarks." The Conscious Lovers; A Comedy, In Five Acts; By Sir Richard Steele. As Performed at the Theatres Royal, Covent Garden and Drury Lane. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers From the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row, pp. 3-7. The British Theatre; or, A Collection of Plays, Which Are Acted At the Theatres Royal, Drury Lane, Covent Garden, and Haymarket. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Books. With Biographical and Critical Remarks, by Mrs. Inchbald. In Twenty-Five Volumes. Vol. XII. Orphan. Venice Preserved. Conscious Lovers. Revenge. Beggar's Opera. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row. 1808. The first performance of this play was staged at the Theatre Royal, Drury Lane on November 7th, 1722. Laura DeWitt and Mary A. Waters edited this essay for The Criticism Archive.
______

div.p: This play is the work of a man of singular character. He was attached to convival, and even dissipated enjoyments; yet the productions of his pen breathe the purest morality, and the chief practice of his life was benevolence, and every other charitable virtue.

Steele is a well known name to the admirers of the Spectator, the Tatler,The Spectator, a collaboration primarily between Richard Steele and Joseph Addison, was a daily publication which ran from March 1st, 1711, to December 6th, 1712, with a second series running from June 18th to December 20th, 1714. The Tatler was a literary and social journal founded by Steele which ran from April 12th, 1709, to January 2nd, 1711. and others of his excellent publications. He was the colleague of the great Addison, in dispensing instruction and amusement to the English nation, who reformed their follies, and even their vices, on the perusal of his able lessons.

The faults, which he saw and regretted in himself, he took infinite pains to eradicate from the inclination of his neighbour. Industriously dissecting his own heart, and beholding each avenue to delinquency, he applied public remedies so efficacious against every minor, as well as capital, error, that his name comes down to posterity as one of the first among British moralists.

Steele was born about the year 1676,Steele was born in 1672 in Dublin, Ireland. in Ireland; in which kingdom a branch of his family was possessed of a considerable estate. His father was of English extraction, but resided in Dublin; was a counsellor there, and private secretary to James, b 2
Duke of Ormond. He sent this, his son Richard, into England for education; where, at the school of the Charter-house, he contracted that intimacy with Addison, his schoolfellow, which lasted, till death dissolved their friendship.Steele's father, the wealthy attorney Richard Steele Senior, died when Steele was four, and his mother passed the next year. Following his parents' deaths, Steele was adopted by his aunt, Lady Katherine Mildmay, and her husband, Henry Gascoigne, secretary to James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde. Gascoigne moved the family to London in the early 1680s, at which point Steele was enrolled in the Charterhouse School, an academically rigorous public school, through Ormonde's influence. It was there that Steele met his lifelong friend and collaborator, Joseph Addison.

The taste of Richard Steele, on arriving at manhood, first led him to the army, and he became an ensign in the Guards, and afterwards a captain in the corps of Fusileers.Steele joined the Life Guards, a ceremonial and fighting outfit of royal bodyguards, in 1692 to support King William III's wars against France. "Fusiliers" are soldiers armed with light muskets—in 1695, Steele joined the Coldstreamers, the second foot guards, and earned the rank of Captain. A taste for polite literature succeeded his military propensity; and he appeared before the public, as a dramatist, in the comedy of "The Funeral; or, Grief a la Mode."The Funeral: or, Grief a-la-Mode, Steele's first play, was first performed in 1701. The comedy was a commercial success and did much to advance Steele's career as a playwright.

In the choice of his pursuits, both as a soldier and an author, he offended his nearest relations, and thereby lost all hopes of succeeding to that property, of which it was in their power to have rendered him possessed;Steele left Oxford University before obtaining his degree to become a soldier. He did so without his uncle's permission, and this disapproval cost him the family estate in West Ireland. but, in return for this serious and substantial good which he inconsiderately relinquished, he found himself the joy, the idol, of his gay companions; and valued such encomiums as his wit and humour excited, beyond every allurement which true affection or sage advice could offer.

His first success as a dramatic writer was soon embittered by a failure in his play of "The Lying Lover."Steele's 1703 comedy The Lying Lover appeared successful upon its first run of six nights, including two benefit performances. However, the play was not revived during his lifetime. But, possessing spirits untamed by disappointment, he pursued his literary employment with renovated ardour, and in his future compositions was generally fortunate.

The "Conscious Lovers" redeemed the credit he had previously forfeited; and in giving the town a novelty, by combining moral instruction with enter-
tainment, he established a reputation with the good, and augmented his sway among the depraved.

This play was performed at Drury Lane in 1721.The first performance of this play was staged at the Theatre Royal, Drury Lane on November 7th, 1722. The merit of the graver scenes, from which the most powerful effect was produced at the time it first appeared, has since been much obscured by imitations which have surpassed the original;The Conscious Lovers popularized the genre of the sentimental comedy, which has produced famous works such as Oliver Goldsmith's She Stoops to Conquer (1773) and Richard Brinsley Sheridan's The Rivals (1775). but to Steele are due the honours of originality, and of teaching an audience to think and to feel, as well as to laugh and applaud, at the representation of a comedy.

The scenes, wherein Bevil and IndianaThe central plot of this play revolves around Bevil Jr. and his father, Sir John Bevil. Sir John forces Bevil Jr. to marry Lucinda, daughter of the wealthy Mr. Sealand. However, Bevil Jr. is in love with Indiana, a poor orphan. Through the play's subplot it is revealed that Indiana is the long-lost daughter of Mr. Sealand by his first wife. are concerned either together or separately, have ever been considered as elegantly written, highly refined, and deeply interesting. There is, notwithstanding, a degree of languor which pervades some of those scenes in the representation; nor has the remaining part of the comedy force sufficient to buoy up those characters, which, upon the stage, sink into insipidity, through the lifeless weight of mere refinement.

But though neither the extravagant raptures of love, nor the brilliancy of wit, are here to be found; sprightly dialogue, nervous sentiment, with affecting incident, are excellent substitutes: and if, in the character of Cimberton,Cimberton is a wealthy young coxcomb whom Mrs. Sealand, Lucinda's mother and Mr. Sealand's second wife, wishes Lucinda to marry. the author has at times degraded his muse, to comply with the degraded taste of the auditors of that period: the readers of this, will pardon such a fault in one, who seldom offended against good manners: and they will surely set a peculiar value on the whole drama, as the work of Sir Richard Steele.

This celebrated author added to his other profes-3
sions, that of a politician; and his pen was of infinite use to the party whose opinions he adopted.Through his popular Whig writings, Steele gained numerous minor political positions, including Gentleman-Waiter to Prince George (1706–8), Gazetteer (1707–10), Commander of Stamps (1710–13), Surveyor of the Royal Stables at Hampton Court (1714–c.1717), Member of Parliament for Stockbridge (in Hampshire, 1713-14), Member of Parliament for Boroughbridge (in Yorkshire, 1715-22), Commander for Forfeited Estates (1716–25), and most notably, Governor of the Theatre Royal, Drury Lane (1715–20). He was indefatigable in producing pamphlets, essays, and other political writings, in support of his friends, and to the annoyance of their adversaries.

As Steele took all the liberty with the press which the press would give, and sometimes a little more—so, as he ranged himself with the strong or with the weak, on the ministerial or on the opposition side of the question, was he alternately punished or rewarded.Steele was known for his extremely outspoken Whig writings and admonishment of the Tory party. The party question was among the most polarizing of Steele's time, and he consistently published Whig writings as the party's power waxed and waned.

About the middle of Queen Anne's reign, this author was both a placeman and pensionerSteele began collecting income from his first wife's estate after her passing in 1706.—towards the end of it, he found the resignation of all such profit necessary; and having procured a seat in parliament, was expelled from that house for writing seditious libels.During his term as Member of Parliament for Stockbridge, Steele published a pamphlet supporting the Hanoverian succession to the throne, as opposed to Queen Anne's Stuart relations. Steele was hence expelled from Parliament on the grounds of "seditious writings."

On the accession of George the First, he was taken into favour, on account of his former disloyalty; was appointed surveyor to the royal stables at Hampton Court; was put into the commission of the peace for the county of Middlesex; obtained a patent from his majesty, which made him manager and governor of the royal company of comedians, during his life, and received the honour of knighthood.Thanks to his previous pamphlet supporting the Hanoverian succession, Steele earned the favor of the newly-crowned King George I, resulting in a knighthood and several political offices.

Sir Richard was now once again chosen a representative in parliament, and obtained a more lucrative appointment than ever, as one of the commissioners for inquiring into the estates, forfeited by the late rebellion in Scotland.The Jacobite Uprising of 1715 was the attempt of James Edward Stuart to reclaim the throne for the Stuart line after the ascension of the Hanoverian King George. As much of the uprising took place in Scotland, many Scottish estates of Jacobites were seized upon the Hanoverian victory. But neither this, the income which arose from his various other sinecure places,
nor a fortune and estate which marriage had brought him, were sufficient to supply that idle luxury in which he lived, and the bounty he bestowed upon the necessitous. He was so inconsiderate, that his very best deeds lost that virtue which they would have derived from premeditation.

Although, from his careless character, the gifts which Steele bestowed upon the poor may be ascribed to his want of thought; still the advice, the admonitions, which he generously gave to the world, can admit of no such conclusion—for he certainly did not write without thinking.
